schools.summary: Ellicott reads 'Satan' without the article as 'an adversary,' identifies it with the wrath of God that let David's pride work out its penal results (cf. Rom. 1:24), and suspects Samuel's text once read 'an adversary incited David'; K&D note the two records draw on one source.
Matthew Henry traces the sin to Satan, whom God permitted for wise and holy ends; God tempts no man, and the devil's chief spite against the church is to tempt its rulers to pride.
Wesley reads Satan's 'standing' as the accuser's posture before God's tribunal; Clarke refers to the parallel in 2 Samuel.

debates.question: How is Chronicles' 'Satan provoked David' to be reconciled with '2 Samuel 24:1's 'the anger of the LORD'?

sides.position: the 'adversary' without the article is the wrath of God, which let David's own pride work its penal results
Satan tempted David, God 'moving' him only by permitting the devil for wise and holy ends

themes: Satan versus the anger of the LORD

schools.summary: K&D note the 'rulers of the people' assisted Joab (2 Sam. 24:4) and the Deut. 1:11 idiom; Ellicott reads the deputing of Joab's staff as proof the census was 'of a military character,' with a verbal echo of Satan's 'roaming' the earth in Job's prologue.

themes: counting the men that draw sword

schools.summary: Ellicott shows the Chronicler recast Samuel's words and added 'why will he be a cause of trespass to Israel'; K&D read Joab's opening question as a loyal disclaimer, not disloyalty.
Henry: Joab remonstrated because the census was needless (God had promised to multiply them) and dangerous; the rough soldier saw the peril the man after God's heart did not.
Wesley: a people is commonly punished for its rulers' sins, being usually guilty in some kind themselves.

themes: a cause of trespass to Israel

schools.summary: Ellicott supplies the omitted itinerary of 2 Sam. 24:4-8; the census 'occupied nine months and twenty days,' and the leisure of Joab and his officers 'indicates a time of settled peace,' dating it to the later years of the reign.

Ellicott weighs three reconciliations of Chronicles' 1,100,000/470,000 with Samuel's 800,000/500,000: round versus exact numbers, a transcription error, varying unarchived tradition (1 Chr. 27:24), or the inclusion of David's ~300,000 standing army (1 Chr. 27:1).
Clarke sets the two sets of figures side by side without harmonizing them.

debates.question: How are Chronicles' census totals (1,100,000 Israel, 470,000 Judah) to be reconciled with Samuel's (800,000 and 500,000)?

sides.position: round versus exact numbers, plus a transcription error or honestly varied, unarchived tradition
Chronicles' larger figure includes David's standing army of ~300,000 (800,000 + 300,000 = 1,100,000)

themes: the proud tally of drawn swords

schools.summary: Clarke reports the rabbins' view that Joab deliberately spared Levi and Benjamin with answers ready; Wesley sets God's providential shielding of the two tribes above Joab's specious pretexts.
Ellicott: Levi was naturally exempt from a military census (Num. 1:47), and 1 Chr. 27:24 says the census was never completed — Joab's disapproval and lack of time (Josephus) left Benjamin uncounted.

debates.question: Why were Levi and Benjamin left uncounted?

sides.position: Joab's deliberate contrivance to save two tribes (the rabbins)
God's gracious providence, sparing the tribe devoted to him and the least, foreseen-faithful tribe
the priestly tribe's natural exemption plus a census simply left incomplete

schools.summary: Henry sets David under the rod that drives out the foolishness of pride; no sin is more displeasing to God than pride of heart, and his dearest children must smart when they sin.
Ellicott: the verse is absent from Samuel (which has 'David's heart smote him') and serves as an anticipative heading to the narrative that follows.
Wesley grounds the displeasure in the deed's needlessness — done out of mere curiosity and ostentation, with no colour of necessity.

debates.question: Wherein lay the sinfulness of the census?

sides.position: pride of heart and self-reliance upon one's own strength
its sheer needlessness, done of curiosity and ostentation

schools.summary: Ellicott: the verse is verbatim with Samuel save for 'God' for 'Jehovah' and the added 'this thing'; 'do away' is 'cause to pass over,' and David's conscience misgave him in the night before Gad came (2 Sam. 24:10-11).
Henry: David made a very penitent confession, owning he had sinned greatly and done very foolishly, and prayed that the iniquity itself be done away — taking the whole weight on himself.

themes: seeking the guilt done away, not just the plague

schools.summary: Ellicott: Samuel's fuller order looks more original; 'David's seer' is better 'a seer of David's' (as Heman, 1 Chr. 25:5); Gad appears in 1 Sam. 22:5 and 1 Chr. 29:29, whence it is inferred that he wrote the original record of the census.

themes: the prophet who wrote the record
the word that both sentences and heals

schools.summary: Ellicott reads the Chronicler's variations as clarifying paraphrase: the imperative 'go,' the added 'saying,' the feminine pronoun, and above all 'I offer thee' (lay before thee) replacing Samuel's 'I lift up' (impose) upon thee — God laying the three judgments before David rather than imposing them.

Ellicott smooths the wording ('Choose thee,' a later Hebrew 'take') and illustrates the triad of penalties from the Annals of Tiglath-Pileser I (c. 1120 B.C.), whose curse invokes the same overthrow of the army, pestilence, and famine.

themes: the covenant scourges of a nation

schools.summary: Ellicott prefers Chronicles' 'three years' famine as harmonizing with the three months and three days, taking Samuel's 'seven' for a scribe's memory of Joseph's famine (Gen. 41:30); the three judgments are all the sword of the LORD (Ezek. 14:21; Lev. 26:25).
Clarke notes the discrepancy: 'three days' of pestilence and three years' famine here, against Samuel's seven years' famine.

debates.question: Was the offered famine to last three years or seven?

sides.position: three years, harmonizing with the three months and three days; Samuel's 'seven' a scribal echo of Joseph's famine
the discrepancy is simply recorded — Samuel reads seven years

schools.summary: Clarke unfolds David's reasoning through the Targum: he rejects famine and war lest men impute callousness, and delivers himself into the hand of the Word of the Lord, whose mercies are many, not into the hands of men.

Ellicott: David confesses his inability to choose, sees only that dependence on God's compassion is better than man's, and so implicitly rejects flight; the pestilence was especially felt to be a divine visitation.

Henry: David cast himself on God's mercy though he knew God was angry, entertaining no hard thoughts — good men, even under God's frown, think well of him ('Though he slay me, yet will I trust in him').

themes: let me fall into the hand of the LORD
not into the hand of man

schools.summary: Ellicott: the first clause stands as in Samuel, the rest abridged; from Samuel we learn the plague raged from dawn to the evening sacrifice.
Wesley: David was proud of the number of his people, and God took a course to make them fewer — justly is that embittered to us which we are proud of.
Henry: God 'numbers them after another manner, numbers to the sword'; the king who demanded a muster-roll receives instead a black bill of mortality of seventy thousand slain.

themes: the muster-roll and the bill of the dead

schools.summary: Ellicott: Samuel's 'the angel stretched out his hand' is probably right; 'as he was destroying, the LORD beheld' softens the transition from command to countermand (Bertheau); Jerusalem seems untouched; 'Ornan' and 'Araunah' are variant spellings of a foreign name.

Clarke reports the Targum's amplification — the angel beheld the ashes of Isaac's binding and God remembered the covenant with Abraham and turned from the evil — a legendary gloss fastening on covenant mercy.
Henry: 'when David repented of the sin God repented of the judgment,' staying the angel's hand and sheathing his sword.

outliers.note: Relays the Targum's legendary expansion (the ashes of Isaac's binding, Jacob's image on the throne of glory) as the reason the angel relented — reported as tradition, not endorsed.

themes: the angel stayed at the threshing-floor

schools.summary: K&D note the Chronicle preserves subordinate details Samuel omits (the drawn sword over Jerusalem); Ellicott ties 'drawn sword' to Num. 22:23 and 'stretched out' to Isaiah 5:25, and reads the sackcloth-clad elders' presence as agreeing with Samuel's 'the king and his servants.'
Wesley: the sackcloth is mourning garb, king and elders humbling themselves and deprecating God's wrath.
Henry dwells on the terror — a flaming sword like the cherubim's; while we lie under God's wrath the holy angels are armed against us, though unseen.

themes: the angel between earth and heaven

schools.summary: K&D: David takes the whole guilt on himself and his father's house, pleading for the innocent flock; Ellicott renders 'these, the sheep' (the king being the shepherd), notes 'done evil indeed' for Samuel's 'dealt crookedly,' and suspects two marginal glosses in the closing clause.
Henry: David accepted the punishment — 'let me be the sufferer, for I am the sinner; mine is the guilty head' — with tender concern for the people, 'These sheep, what have they done?'

themes: these sheep, what have they done

schools.summary: Henry: the same angel that waged the war now sets on foot the treaty of peace, sending the order through the seer to honour the prophetic office (as the angel notified John, Rev. 1:1); the command to build an altar is 'a blessed token of reconciliation.'

Ellicott renders 'the angel had told Gad to tell David' and notes that angelic mediation between God and prophets, absent from Samuel here, is a mark of the later canonical books (Dan. 8:16; Zech. 1:9).
Wesley: the command to build an altar is a token of reconciliation, for God would not have commanded a sacrifice he did not mean to accept.

Ellicott: 'at the saying' differs from Samuel's 'according to' only by 'the one tittle' of Matt. 5:18, and 'in the name of the LORD' is a merely verbal variation on 'as the LORD commanded' — the two accounts of David's obedience agreeing all but to a letter.

Ellicott judges the text corrupt and follows Samuel's 'saw the king and his servants,' the Hebrew for 'angel'/'king' being easily confused; Keil and Delitzsch defend the Masoretic 'turned and saw the angel,' faulting Bertheau's conjectures. Both accept 'now Ornan was threshing wheat.'
Clarke reviews the versions (LXX, Syriac, Houbigant, a Kennicott MS) reading 'the king,' with Houbigant making v.20 Ornan's distant sight of David and v.21 the meeting.
Henry takes 'saw the angel' as it stands: the sight so terrified Ornan and his four sons that they hid, and the God-fearing will do all they can toward reconciliation.

debates.question: Did Ornan turn and see 'the angel' or 'the king'?

sides.position: 'the angel,' with the Masoretic text
'the king,' the text of Samuel and the versions being right

themes: angel or king, a textual crux
Ornan and his four sons hiding

schools.summary: K&D present the meeting as the encounter that sets the purchase in motion; Ellicott notes the clause is wanting in Samuel — the corruption of v.20 'made some such statement necessary' — and otherwise corresponds to 2 Sam. 24:20.

themes: the threshold of the temple site

schools.summary: Ellicott: Chronicles compresses Samuel; 'at a full price give it me' (not in Samuel) echoes Abraham's purchase of Machpelah (Gen. 23:9); K&D give David's aim — the place at full price, that the plague be stayed.
Henry: David 'would not serve God at other people's charge,' making a fair bargain even amid the plague.

themes: asking the floor at full price
that the plague may be stayed
not serving God at others' charge

schools.summary: Ellicott hears Ephron's courtesy (Gen. 23:11) in 'Take it to thee,' glosses the rare 'threshing instruments' (moraq; Isa. 41:15; 2 Sam. 24:22), and notes 'the wheat for the meat offering' is probably original though Samuel omits it and adds Araunah's blessing; K&D render Ornan's whole lavish offer.
Henry: Ornan offered it gratis, moved not only by deference to the king but because he had seen the angel and would do anything toward the atonement.

Ellicott: 'for the full price' answers Samuel's 'at a price,' and cites Ewald that 'it was of the essence of sacrifice to surrender something valued to win from God a greater good'; K&D give David's resolve not to offer what costs him nothing.
Henry: David 'would not serve God at other people's charge.'

themes: I will not offer that which costs me nothing

schools.summary: Ellicott calls the two estimates 'obviously discordant': fifty silver too little, six hundred gold too high (cf. Machpelah, Gen. 23:16); perhaps read 'silver' for 'gold,' or Samuel priced the floor and Chronicles the whole Mount Moriah — ours 'probably the later form.' K&D refer to their Samuel discussion.
Wesley harmonizes: fifty shekels of gold (Samuel) is the value of six hundred shekels of silver — the same sum in different metals.

debates.question: How are Samuel's fifty shekels of silver and Chronicles' six hundred shekels of gold to be reconciled?

sides.position: they name one sum in two metals — fifty of gold equal six hundred of silver
a scribal 'gold' for 'silver,' or the two texts price different things (floor versus the whole temple mount); Chronicles the later tradition
see the fuller discussion on 2 Samuel 24:24

themes: fifty of silver versus six hundred of gold
floor or the whole Mount Moriah

schools.summary: K&D and Ellicott read the fire from heaven as the divine inauguration of the new altar and the sign that David's prayer was heard, the pattern attested at Lev. 9:24, 1 Kings 18:38, and 2 Chron. 7:1.
Clarke: the fire showed God accepted David and was pacified; Wesley: the fire that might justly have fastened on the sinner fastened on the sacrifice — 'thus Christ was made sin and a curse for us.'
Henry, in nearly Wesley's words, reads the answering fire as substitution: it fell on the sacrifice, not the sinner, so that Christ was made sin and a curse for us.

themes: fire on the sacrifice, not the sinner

schools.summary: K&D read the sheathed sword as a figurative expression for the stopping of the plague; Ellicott resists calling it merely figurative, insisting (against Reuss) that the angel is 'a real concrete being,' and notes the rare 'sheath' (nadan) is elsewhere the sheath of the soul (Dan. 7:15).

debates.question: Is the sword-sheathing angel a real being or only a personification of the plague's cessation?

sides.position: the sheathed sword figuratively marks the plague's end, the angel a real messenger
the angel is a mere personification, not a concrete being

themes: the sword put up into its sheath
a real angel, not a personification

schools.summary: Gill identifies Ornan with Araunah (a small variation of letters, both meaning the ash/pine per Hillerus) and recalls that God accepted David's prayer here by fire and by sheathing the angel's sword.
Ellicott: the floor's use as a place of sacrifice continued from the plague's cessation; the fire from heaven (v.26) was 'an unmistakable intimation of the Divine will' (cf. Josh. 5:15).
Wesley: perceiving his sacrifice acceptable, David proceeded to offer more sacrifices in that place.
Gill: the tabernacle 'which Moses made' — ordered to be made by God's command and according to his direction.
Ellicott: 'the tabernacle,' the recognized dwelling-place of Jehovah, is named in deliberate contrast with Ornan's threshingfloor, 'the new sanctuary.'

themes: the Mosaic altar of burnt offering

schools.summary: Gill: David could not go before the tabernacle to inquire of God, though that was the proper place to seek him — the verse supplies its own reason.

Clarke: this is why David built at Ornan's floor — fear of the angel's sword, or of a fatal delay, God sealing the improvised altar by fire; Wesley: David durst not leave for Gibeon lest the angel destroy Jerusalem meanwhile.

Ellicott canvasses the views — Keil's plague raging at Gibeon, a bodily weakness, or (his own preference) the apparition overawing David with a sense of the place's sanctity (cf. Gen. 28:17; Exod. 3:5).

debates.question: Why could David not go to Gibeon to inquire of God?

sides.position: the pestilence was raging at Gibeon
the peril of delay — he durst not leave the angel poised over Jerusalem
the apparition overawed him with the sanctity of the new place

themes: why David did not go to Gibeon
the fear of the angel's sword
the improvised altar sealed by fire
the threshing-floor becomes the temple ground
